title: Basic Nursing Care — Infection Control & Precautions (Set 4)
subtitle: Hand hygiene, standard & transmission-based precautions, PPE, sharps & isolation
scenario: Preventing the spread of infection through hand hygiene, standard and transmission-based precautions, personal protective equipment, safe sharps handling, and supportive isolation care.

steps.q: The SINGLE most effective way to prevent the spread of infection is:

opts.text: Hand hygiene
opts.rat: Proper hand hygiene is the single most effective measure to prevent the transmission of infection.

opts.text: Wearing a gown at all times
opts.rat: Gowns are used situationally; hand hygiene is the foundational measure.

opts.text: Keeping the lights dim
opts.rat: Lighting has no effect on infection transmission.

opts.text: Playing soft music
opts.rat: This is unrelated to infection control.

steps.q: Standard precautions should be used:

opts.text: Only for clients known to have an infection
opts.rat: Standard precautions apply to everyone, since infection status is not always known.

opts.text: With every client, treating all blood and body fluids as potentially infectious
opts.rat: Standard precautions apply to all clients and treat blood and body fluids as potentially infectious, regardless of diagnosis.

opts.text: Only during flu season
opts.rat: Standard precautions are used year-round with all clients.

opts.text: Only in the emergency department
opts.rat: They apply in all care settings.

steps.q: When should the technician perform hand hygiene?

opts.text: Only at the start of the shift
opts.rat: Hand hygiene is required at many points, not just once per shift.

opts.text: Only if the hands look dirty
opts.rat: Hands can carry pathogens even when they appear clean.

opts.text: Before and after client contact and after removing gloves
opts.rat: Hand hygiene is performed before and after client contact, after contact with body fluids, and after removing gloves, among other moments.

opts.text: Only after using the restroom
opts.rat: That is one of many required times, not the only one.

steps.q: When hands are visibly soiled, the technician should clean them with:

opts.text: Alcohol-based hand sanitizer only
opts.rat: Alcohol sanitizer does not remove visible soil and does not kill certain spores.

opts.text: A dry paper towel alone
opts.rat: Wiping does not disinfect soiled hands.

opts.text: Lotion
opts.rat: Lotion does not clean or disinfect the hands.

opts.text: Soap and water
opts.rat: Visibly soiled hands must be washed with soap and water; alcohol-based sanitizer is not sufficient for visible soil.

steps.q: The correct order for REMOVING personal protective equipment (PPE) generally begins with:

opts.text: Removing the gloves first (the most contaminated item)
opts.rat: Gloves are removed first because they are the most contaminated, followed by goggles/face shield, gown, then mask, with hand hygiene afterward.

opts.text: Removing the mask first
opts.rat: The mask/respirator is removed last, after leaving the room.

opts.text: Removing the gown before the gloves
opts.rat: Gloves come off before the gown to avoid contaminating the hands.

opts.text: There is no particular order
opts.rat: There is a specific sequence to prevent self-contamination.

steps.q: A client has a wound infected with MRSA. Which type of transmission-based precaution is required?

opts.text: No extra precautions
opts.rat: MRSA requires contact precautions beyond standard precautions.

opts.text: Contact precautions (gown and gloves)
opts.rat: MRSA and similar organisms spread by contact require contact precautions—gown and gloves—plus dedicated or disinfected equipment.

opts.text: Airborne precautions with an N95
opts.rat: MRSA wound infection spreads by contact, not the airborne route.

opts.text: Only a surgical mask
opts.rat: A mask alone does not address contact transmission.

steps.q: Which precaution is required for a client with active pulmonary tuberculosis (TB)?

opts.text: Contact precautions only
opts.rat: TB is airborne, not primarily contact-spread.

opts.text: No precautions beyond standard
opts.rat: Active TB requires airborne precautions.

opts.text: Airborne precautions with an N95 respirator and a negative-pressure room
opts.rat: TB spreads via airborne droplet nuclei, requiring airborne precautions: an N95 (fit-tested) respirator and a negative-pressure room with the door closed.

opts.text: A cloth face covering is sufficient
opts.rat: A cloth covering does not filter airborne droplet nuclei; an N95 is required.

steps.q: Droplet precautions (e.g., for influenza) require the technician to:

opts.text: Wear an N95 and use a negative-pressure room
opts.rat: That is airborne precautions, more than droplet precautions require.

opts.text: Use no protective equipment at all
opts.rat: A mask is needed for droplet precautions.

opts.text: Wear only gloves
opts.rat: Gloves alone do not protect against droplet spread; a mask is required.

opts.text: Wear a surgical mask when within close range of the client
opts.rat: Droplet precautions require a surgical mask when in close proximity, since droplets travel short distances through the air.

steps.q: For a client with Clostridioides difficile (C. diff), the technician should perform hand hygiene by:

opts.text: Washing with soap and water rather than relying on alcohol sanitizer
opts.rat: C. diff spores are not reliably killed by alcohol sanitizer; hands must be washed with soap and water, and contact precautions are used.

opts.text: Using alcohol-based sanitizer only
opts.rat: Alcohol sanitizer does not kill C. diff spores.

opts.text: Skipping hand hygiene if gloves were worn
opts.rat: Hand hygiene is still required after removing gloves.

opts.text: Wiping hands on a towel only
opts.rat: This does not remove spores.

steps.q: After giving an injection, the technician should dispose of the needle by:

opts.text: Recapping it and putting it in the trash
opts.rat: Recapping increases needlestick risk and the regular trash is unsafe for sharps.

opts.text: Placing it uncapped into a puncture-resistant sharps container
opts.rat: Needles are placed directly into a puncture-resistant sharps container without recapping, to prevent needlestick injury.

opts.text: Bending the needle and pocketing it
opts.rat: This is extremely unsafe and prohibited.

opts.text: Leaving it on the bedside table
opts.rat: Leaving a sharp exposed endangers clients and staff.

steps.q: To prevent a needlestick injury, the technician should:

opts.text: Always recap needles with two hands
opts.rat: Two-handed recapping greatly increases injury risk.

opts.text: Carry used needles in a pocket
opts.rat: This is extremely dangerous.

opts.text: Never recap a used needle by hand
opts.rat: Recapping used needles by hand is a leading cause of needlestick injuries and should be avoided; sharps go directly into the container.

opts.text: Hand used needles to a coworker
opts.rat: Passing exposed sharps risks injury; they go into the sharps container.

steps.q: When handling soiled linens, the technician should:

opts.text: Shake them out to loosen debris
opts.rat: Shaking linens spreads microorganisms into the air.

opts.text: Hug them against the uniform
opts.rat: Holding soiled linens against the body contaminates the uniform.

opts.text: Place them on the floor first
opts.rat: The floor is contaminated and this spreads organisms.

opts.text: Hold them away from the body and avoid shaking them
opts.rat: Soiled linens are held away from the uniform and not shaken, to avoid dispersing microorganisms into the air and onto the technician.

steps.q: Respiratory hygiene / cough etiquette includes teaching clients to:

opts.text: Cover coughs/sneezes and perform hand hygiene afterward
opts.rat: Covering coughs and sneezes (with a tissue or the elbow) and cleaning the hands afterward reduces the spread of respiratory infections.

opts.text: Cough openly into the air near others
opts.rat: Uncovered coughing spreads respiratory droplets.

opts.text: Never wash hands after coughing
opts.rat: Hand hygiene after coughing is part of respiratory etiquette.

opts.text: Share used tissues
opts.rat: Sharing tissues spreads infection.

steps.q: A client in isolation for an infection becomes tearful and says they feel lonely and 'like a leper.' The technician should:

opts.text: Avoid the room to save time
opts.rat: Avoiding the client worsens isolation and neglects care.

opts.text: Provide emotional support and regular interaction while maintaining precautions
opts.rat: Isolation can cause loneliness and low mood; the technician should offer psychosocial support and frequent, warm interaction while following precautions.

opts.text: Tell the client to stop complaining
opts.rat: This is dismissive and non-therapeutic.

opts.text: Remove the precautions to make the client feel better
opts.rat: Precautions must be maintained for safety; support is provided within them.

steps.q: Which finding suggests a client may have a localized infection that the technician should report?

opts.text: Pink, intact, cool skin
opts.rat: This describes healthy skin, not infection.

opts.text: A normal temperature and no drainage
opts.rat: These are reassuring findings, not signs of infection.

opts.text: Redness, warmth, swelling, and purulent (pus) drainage at a site
opts.rat: Redness, warmth, swelling, and purulent drainage are classic signs of a localized infection and should be reported.

opts.text: A well-healed, dry scar
opts.rat: A healed scar is not a sign of active infection.

steps.q: Gloves should be changed:

opts.text: Only once per shift
opts.rat: Wearing the same gloves all shift spreads contamination.

opts.text: Never, if they still look intact
opts.rat: Gloves are changed regardless of appearance to prevent cross-contamination.

opts.text: Only when visibly torn
opts.rat: Gloves are changed between clients/tasks, not only when torn.

opts.text: Between clients and between tasks (e.g., dirty to clean) on the same client
opts.rat: Gloves are changed between clients and between a contaminated and a clean task on the same client, with hand hygiene in between.

steps.q: An immunocompromised (e.g., very low white-blood-cell count) client may need protective (reverse) isolation, which is intended to:

opts.text: Protect the client from organisms carried by others
opts.rat: Protective (reverse) isolation shields a highly susceptible, immunocompromised client from pathogens that visitors and staff may carry.

opts.text: Protect staff from the client
opts.rat: That is the purpose of standard/transmission-based precautions, not protective isolation.

opts.text: Punish the client
opts.rat: Isolation is never punitive.

opts.text: Save money on supplies
opts.rat: This is unrelated to the purpose of protective isolation.

steps.q: The 'chain of infection' can be interrupted by the technician when they:

opts.text: Encourage sharing of personal items between clients
opts.rat: Sharing items promotes, rather than blocks, transmission.

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and follow precautions to block transmission
opts.rat: Breaking any link in the chain of infection—most readily the mode of transmission, via hand hygiene and precautions—prevents spread.

opts.text: Skip cleaning shared equipment
opts.rat: Not cleaning equipment allows transmission to continue.

opts.text: Reuse disposable supplies between clients
opts.rat: Reusing single-use supplies spreads infection.

steps.q: When a small blood spill occurs on a surface, the technician should:

opts.text: Leave it for the next shift
opts.rat: Blood spills must be cleaned promptly for safety.

opts.text: Wipe it with a bare hand
opts.rat: Bare-handed contact with blood is unsafe; gloves are required.

opts.text: Wear gloves and clean/disinfect the spill per facility policy
opts.rat: Blood spills are cleaned while wearing gloves and disinfected with an appropriate agent per policy, treating the blood as potentially infectious.

opts.text: Cover it with a blanket
opts.rat: Covering does not clean or disinfect the spill.

steps.q: To reduce the risk of transmitting infection, the technician should:

opts.text: Wear long artificial nails for direct care
opts.rat: Artificial/long nails harbor more microorganisms and are discouraged in direct care.

opts.text: Skip hand hygiene if wearing rings
opts.rat: Hand hygiene is always required; rings can harbor organisms.

opts.text: Avoid changing gloves to save supplies
opts.rat: Not changing gloves spreads infection.

opts.text: Keep fingernails short and avoid artificial nails when giving direct care
opts.rat: Short natural nails (avoiding artificial nails) harbor fewer organisms and support effective hand hygiene during direct client care.
